content.full_text:
Fern Polrrlcal PnacrrcEs CorraurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
September 23,2003
Lori Jacobs
Scott & Cronin, LLP
330 Encinitas Blvd., Suite 101
Encinitas, CA 92024-3723
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4.-03-208
CONCLUSIONS
Yes. Expenditures of this type made by a sponsoring organization on
behalf of its committee are not counted toward qualifying as a committee
if the expenses are reported by the committee and aresponsible officer of
' Gor"rnment Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
i8109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
Dear Ms. Jacobs:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the San Diego
Board of Realtors IMPAC committee regarding the campaign provisions of the political
Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTTONS
May the san Diego Board of Realtors (the "Board") pay the expenses for a
fundraiser held on behalf of the San Diego Board of Realtors IMPAC
committee, a committee sponsored by the Board, without it quali$ring as a
separate committee? If so, is this because the Board is supporting its own
committee and not making a contribution to a candidate or other
committee?
How are the payments made by the Board on behalf of the committee in
support of the fundraiser reported by the committee?
For bookkeeping purposes, how are receipts of under $25 from a single
source noted?
1
2.
J.
1
,1€a-


File No. 03-208
Page No. 2
the Board verifies the committee's campaign statement, in addition to the
committee's treasurer. This exception to the definition of a committee
would not apply if the Board makes contributions to candidates or
committees and the payments are not reported on the committee's
campaign statement, or a responsible officer of the Board does not verify
the staternent.
These types of payments by the sponsor of a committee are reported as
nonmonetary contributions on the committee's campaign statement Form
460, Schedule C.
Receipts of under $25 from a single source are noted in the committee's
records as a lump sum amount. No additional information on the source
of the receipts is required
FACTS
The San Diego Board of Realtors recently held a fundraiser on behalf of its
sponsored committee. You wish to clarify whether the Board may pay the expenses for
the fundraiser without making a qualifying contribution that would count toward the
Board itself becoming a recipient committee. In addition, the fundraiser included the sale
of raffle tickets, each sold for under $25.
ANALYSIS
Ouestion I
A recipient committee is defined in section 82013 as:
"...arry person or combination ofpersons who directlyor
indirectly ...
(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar year."
A contribution is defined in section 82015(a), in part, as:
"... a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payrnent of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a
payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances thatit is not made for political
purposes."
A payment is made for "political purposes" if it is:
"For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against the nomination or
2
aJ


File No. 03-208
Page No. 3
election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or
passage of any measure." (Regulation 18215(a)(1).)
In addition, the Commission relies on regulation 18215(b)(1) to define certain
multi-purpose organizations, such as the Board, as a recipient committee. This regulation
defines the term "contribution," in part, as:
"Any payment made to a person or organization other than
a candidate or committee, when, at the time of making the
payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the
payment, or funds with which the payrnent will be
commingled, will be used to make contributions or
expenditures. If the donor knows or has reason to know
that only part of the payment will be used to make
contributions or expenditures, the payment shall be
apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to determine the
amount of the contribution.
There shall be a presumption that the donor does not have
reason to know that all or part of the payment will be used
to make expenditures or contributions, unless the person or
organization has made expenditures or contributions of at
least one thousand dollars ($1,000) in the aggregate during
the calendar year in which the payment occurs, or any of
the immediately preceding four calendar years."
Thus, if the Board receives $1,000 or more in a calendar year for political
pu{poses, or makes contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar yeff as defined in
regulation 18215(bX1), it will qualiff as a recipient committee. However, there is an
exception to this rule for the sponsor of a committee.
A sponsor of a committee is defined as:
"... arry person, except a candidate, proponent, or other
individual to whom any of the following applies:
(A) The committee receives 80 percent ormore of its
contributions either from the person or frorn the person's
members, officers, employees or shareholders;
(B) The person collects contributions for the committee by
use of payroll deductions or dues form its members,
officers or employees;
(C) The person provides, alone or in combination with
other organizations, all or nearly all of the administrative
services for the committee; or
(D) The person sets, alone or in combination with other
organizations, the policies for soliciting contributions or


FileNo.03-208
Page No. 4
making expenditures of committee funds." (Regulation
tsate@)(2xA-D).)
Regulation 18a19(c)(1-4) provides the criteria under which the sponsor of a
committee will not quali8z as a separate committee. That regulation states, in relevant
part,that the sponsor will not become a committee if:
"(1) The sponsor does not directly or indirectly make or
receive a sufficient amount of contributions or independent
expenditures, other than those in support of its sponsored
committee, to satisff the thresholds set forth in
Government Code Section 82013. A sponsoring
organization makes contributions and expenditures in
support of its sponsored committee when it provides the
committee with member contributions or money from its
treasury, with the exception of establishment or
administrative costs (see Title 2, Califomia Code of
Regulations, section 1 821 5(c)(16));
(2)The sponsored committee reports all contributions and
expenditures made in support of the committee by the
sponsor, its intermediate units, and the members of such
entities. With respect to a member contribution which is
channeled through the sponsor or an intermediate unit, the
member is the contributor;
ti ... 1T
(4) A responsible officer of the sponsor, as well as the
treasurer of the sponsored committee, verifies the
committee's campaign statement pursuant to Govemment
Code Section 81004."
Thus, if the San Diego Board of Realtors IMPAC committee reports the payments
made by the Board on the committee's behalf and a responsible officer of the sponsor, as
well as the treasurer of the committee, signs the committee's report, the Board will not
become a separate committee. However, if the Board makes contributions to other
committees that are not made on behalf of the committee, not reported by the committee,
or the committee's campaign report is not signed by a responsible officer of the Board,
the Board may become a separate committee.
Question 2
Contributions, including nonmonetary contributions, of $100 or more from a
single source in a calendar year must be itemized on the committee's campaign
statement. (Section 84211.) The committee must report the following information on the
contributor:


File No. 03-208
Page No. 5
"(1) His or her full name.
(2) His or her street address.
(3) His or her occupation.
(a) The name of his or her employer, or if self-employed,
the name of the business.
(5) The date and amount received for each contribution
received during the period covered by the campaign
statement and if the contribution is a loan, the interest
rate for the loan.
(6) The cumulative amount of contributions.,' (Section
8421l(DO-6).)
A nonmonetary contribution is defined in section 82015(b)(1) as:
'A payment made at the behest of a committee ... is a
contribution to the committee unless full and adequate
consideration is received from the committee for making
the payment."
Therefore, when the Board makes payments on behalf of the committee, and the
committee does not provide "full and adequate consideration," i.e., the committee does
not reimburse the Board for its expenses, the Board has made a nonmonetary contribution
to the committee. Nonmonetary contributions are reported on Schedule C of the Form
460' Regulation 18215(c)(16) provides a pertinent exception to the definition of a
contribution.
"A pa5rmentby a sponsoring organization for the
establishment and administration of a sponsored
committee, provided such payments are reported. Any
monetarypayment made under this subdivision to the
sponsored committee shall be made by separate
instrument. . ..'Establishment and administration, means
the cost of office space, phones, salaries, utilities, supplies,
legal and accounting fees, and other expenses incurred in
setting up and running a sponsored committee.,,
Thus, payments made by the Board for "establishment and administration" of its
sponsored committee do not count against any applicable contribution limits.
Neverthless, the committee must report the payments for these services on Schedule C as
a memo entry.


File No. 03-208
Page No. 6
Ouestion 3
Regulation 18401, subdivision (aXl), requires the maintenance of detailed
information and original source documentation, as follows:
'?or a contribution received or other receipt of less than $25, or an
expenditure made of less than $25:
(A) The accounts and records shall contain a continuous
computation of campaign account balances, and also shall
include a listing reflecting the dates and daily totals of such
contributions, other receipts, or expenditures on the dates of
such contributions, other receipts or expenditures.
(B) The original source documentation shall consist of all bank
statements, check registers, check stubs, bank or passbooks, and
any other records reflecting a continuous computation of
campaign account balances in any savings or checking account,
money market account, certificate of deposit, credit card
account, or any other campaign account, in any bank or other
financial institution."
The regulation does not require any additional information or information on any
contributor of less than $25.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (e16)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
KSMjg
I:\Adviceltrs\03-208

money market account, certificate of deposit, credit card
account, or any other campaign account, in any bank or other
financial institution."
The regulation does not require any additional information or information on any
contributor of less than $25.

purposes."

QUESTION: 2
Contributions, including nonmonetary contributions, of $100 or more from a
single source in a calendar year must be itemized on the committee's campaign
statement. (Section 84211.) The committee must report the following information on the
contributor:
"(1) His or her full name.
(2) His or her street address.
(3) His or her occupation.
(a) The name of his or her employer, or if self-employed,
the name of the business.
(5) The date and amount received for each contribution
received during the period covered by the campaign
statement and if the contribution is a loan, the interest
rate for the loan.
(6) The cumulative amount of contributions.,' (Section
8421l(DO-6).)
A nonmonetary contribution is defined in section 82015(b)(1) as:
'A payment made at the behest of a committee ... is a
contribution to the committee unless full and adequate
consideration is received from the committee for making
the payment."
Therefore, when the Board makes payments on behalf of the committee, and the
committee does not provide "full and adequate consideration," i.e., the committee does
not reimburse the Board for its expenses, the Board has made a nonmonetary contribution
to the committee. Nonmonetary contributions are reported on Schedule C of the Form
460' Regulation 18215(c)(16) provides a pertinent exception to the definition of a
contribution.
"A pa5rmentby a sponsoring organization for the
establishment and administration of a sponsored
committee, provided such payments are reported. Any
monetarypayment made under this subdivision to the
sponsored committee shall be made by separate
instrument. . ..'Establishment and administration, means
the cost of office space, phones, salaries, utilities, supplies,
legal and accounting fees, and other expenses incurred in
setting up and running a sponsored committee.,,
Thus, payments made by the Board for "establishment and administration" of its
sponsored committee do not count against any applicable contribution limits.
Neverthless, the committee must report the payments for these services on Schedule C as
a memo entry.
Ouestion 3
Regulation 18401, subdivision (aXl), requires the maintenance of detailed
information and original source documentation, as follows:
'?or a contribution received or other receipt of less than $25, or an
expenditure made of less than $25:
(A) The accounts and records shall contain a continuous
computation of campaign account balances, and also shall
include a listing reflecting the dates and daily totals of such
contributions, other receipts, or expenditures on the dates of
such contributions, other receipts or expenditures.
(B) The original source documentation shall consist of all bank
statements, check registers, check stubs, bank or passbooks, and
any other records reflecting a continuous computation of
campaign account balances in any savings or checking account,
money market account, certificate of deposit, credit card
account, or any other campaign account, in any bank or other
financial institution."
The regulation does not require any additional information or information on any
contributor of less than $25.

behalf of its committee are not counted toward qualifying as a committee
if the expenses are reported by the committee and aresponsible officer of
